name: Milk Craft Mastery Programme
purpose: Integrates milk steaming technique (B043), alternative milk handling (B044), and latte art (B045) into a comprehensive milk craft mastery programme that develops advanced skills, builds team capability, and positions milk quality as a defining feature of the cafe's identity.

detailed_objectives: Create a unified milk craft training programme covering dairy, plant-based, and presentation skills
Develop advanced barista capabilities beyond the fundamentals of each component block
Establish milk craft as a team-wide competency rather than an individual talent
Build structured practice and assessment routines that maintain and elevate standards over time
Position the cafe's milk craft as a visible differentiator in the local market
secondary_objectives: Reduce overall milk waste across dairy and alternative categories through improved technique
Create career development opportunities linked to milk craft skill progression
Generate consistent social media content from high-quality drink presentation
Inform menu development decisions based on what the team can consistently deliver at a high level

why_this_matters: Milk is the canvas and the medium for the majority of drinks a UK cafe serves. Yet most cafes treat milk skills as something baristas either naturally possess or gradually absorb — there is no structured development programme. This creates wide quality variation between team members, with customers experiencing a different drink depending on who makes it. The cafes that excel in milk craft do so because they invest in it deliberately: structured training, regular practice, ongoing assessment, and a culture that values the skill. A mastery programme elevates milk craft from a set of individual techniques into a cohesive discipline. When a barista understands not just how to steam milk but why different milks behave differently, how temperature affects sweetness, how foam density changes the espresso-milk integration, and how pouring technique creates visual contrast, they can adapt to any situation — a new alternative milk product, a different cup size, a challenging latte art request. This adaptability is the hallmark of mastery versus mere competence. For the cafe, the commercial benefits are substantial. Consistent milk quality reduces waste, increases customer satisfaction, generates social media content, and builds a reputation that chain competitors cannot replicate. A barista who has progressed through a mastery programme feels invested, valued, and more likely to stay — directly impacting retention in an industry plagued by turnover.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Review the status of B043, B044, and B045 — this mastery programme requires all three foundation blocks to be active and functioning. Identify any gaps in the team's current milk skills: are there baristas who can steam dairy beautifully but struggle with oat? Who can pour a heart but not a rosetta? Who avoids alternative milks because they lack confidence? Map the team's current skill levels across all three dimensions: dairy steaming, alternative milk handling, and latte art. Create a simple skills matrix showing each barista's level in each area. This visual overview immediately highlights where the team is strong and where development is needed. Week 2: Based on the skills matrix, create individual development plans for each barista. These should be collaborative — discuss with each person what they want to improve, not just what you need them to improve. Set achievable 30-day goals for each person. Establish a weekly 'milk craft practice' session of 30 minutes, ideally during a quieter trading period, where the team practises together. This is different from individual practice during service — it is focused, structured development time where baristas can experiment, make mistakes, and learn from each other without the pressure of a customer waiting.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a formal Milk Craft Mastery Programme document that integrates skills from B043, B044, and B045 into a single progression pathway with four levels: Beginner (can steam dairy to temperature, pours without art), Competent (consistent dairy steaming and heart pours, can steam oat milk), Proficient (rosetta pours on dairy and oat, handles all alternative milks confidently, manages waste effectively), and Master (advanced patterns on dairy and oat, can train others, troubleshoots independently, contributes to recipe development). Create practical assessments for each level that baristas complete during observed practice sessions, not during busy service. Build a practice log where baristas record their practice time, techniques worked on, and self-assessment — this builds reflective habits and demonstrates progression. Establish a mentoring system: pair each developing barista with a more experienced team member for regular practice sessions. Introduce monthly milk craft challenges that test all three dimensions: a steaming accuracy test (temperature and texture), an alternative milk identification test (taste three milks blind and identify them), and a latte art challenge. Track waste data by barista and correlate with skill level — this provides a financial justification for training investment. Create an onboarding module so new starters are introduced to the milk craft programme from day one.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Milk craft mastery should now be embedded in the cafe's culture. Celebrate skill progression publicly — announce when a barista achieves a new level, and make it part of the cafe's story. Introduce cross-venue practice sessions with other quality-focused cafes in the area — this builds community, exposes the team to different approaches, and prevents insularity. Enter regional and national competitions as a team, not just individual entrants. Develop signature milk techniques unique to the cafe — perhaps a specific microfoam density that creates a distinctive mouthfeel, or a signature art pattern. Track the programme's impact on customer satisfaction, social media engagement, staff retention, and waste reduction — this data justifies ongoing investment. Review and update the programme annually, incorporating new alternative milk products, new techniques, and lessons from competitions and cross-venue exchanges.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review B043, B044, and B045 status and address any gaps
Map the team's current skill levels across dairy steaming, alternative milks, and latte art
Create a skills matrix showing each barista's level in each dimension
Develop individual 30-day development plans collaboratively with each barista
Establish a weekly 30-minute structured milk craft practice session
Identify skill gaps and pair baristas for peer learning
Set achievable, measurable goals for each team member

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop the formal four-level Milk Craft Mastery Programme document
Create practical assessments for each level
Build a practice log system for barista self-tracking
Establish a mentoring system pairing developing and experienced baristas
Introduce monthly multi-dimensional milk craft challenges
Track waste data by barista and correlate with skill level progression
Create a milk craft onboarding module for new starters

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Celebrate skill progression publicly within the team and on social media
Organise cross-venue practice sessions with other quality-focused cafes
Enter regional and national competitions as a team
Develop signature milk techniques unique to the cafe
Track programme impact on satisfaction, engagement, retention, and waste
Review and update the programme annually

deliverables: Milk Craft Mastery Programme document with four skill levels
Practical Assessment checklists for each level

tools.description: Visual matrix showing each barista's current skill level across dairy steaming, alternative milk handling, and latte art. Updated monthly and used for development planning.
Personal development plan for each barista covering current skill levels, 30-day goals, practice focus areas, and mentor assignment. Reviewed and updated monthly.
Complete assessment pack with practical test criteria for four mastery levels. Covers dairy steaming accuracy, alternative milk competence, latte art quality, waste management, and training ability.
Personal log where baristas record practice sessions: date, duration, techniques practised, self-assessment of results, and areas for improvement. Reviewed during monthly development conversations.
Quarterly report tracking programme outcomes: skill progression rates, waste reduction, customer satisfaction trends, social media engagement from milk craft content, and staff retention correlation.

failure_modes: The programme is overly formal and bureaucratic, turning skill development into a chore rather than a pleasure
Practice sessions are consistently cancelled due to staffing pressures, undermining the development culture
Assessment becomes stressful rather than developmental, discouraging baristas from participating
The programme focuses on the head barista's standards without incorporating diverse team perspectives and strengths
kpis: 100% of baristas achieve Competent level within three months of starting
At least 60% of baristas reach Proficient level within twelve months
Weekly practice sessions held at least 80% of scheduled weeks
Milk waste reduced by 15% within six months of programme launch
constraints: Practice sessions require protected time — management must commit to not cancelling for staffing convenience
Assessments should be conducted during practice, not during busy service
The programme must be adapted to the cafe's positioning — not every venue needs Master-level skills

time_load.L1: 3 hours (skills mapping, development planning, first practice session)
time_load.L2: 10 hours (programme documentation, assessment creation, mentoring setup, tracking systems)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/month (practice sessions, challenges, assessments, cross-venue activities)

review_protocol: Weekly practice sessions. Monthly individual development plan reviews. Monthly milk craft challenges. Quarterly programme impact assessment. Annual programme review and update.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block integrates dairy steaming, alternative milk handling, and latte art into a unified mastery programme with four skill levels, structured practice, mentoring, and ongoing assessment. It transforms individual milk skills into a team-wide competency and cultural identity.
implementation_risks: Pursuing mastery before foundations are solid creates frustration — ensure B043, B044, B045 are established first
Assessment anxiety can undermine the developmental intent if not managed sensitively
The programme may become the head barista's project and lose momentum if they leave
meta.notes_for_engine: Only activate after B043, B044, and B045 are established at L2. This is a maturity block. Signals for activation: strong foundation blocks, team interest in development, owner commitment to quality. Pairs with B042 for a comprehensive craft development programme.
